abstract: In response to salinity stress, plants must manage the uptake and internal distribution of Na⁺ to balance osmotic requirements against the risk of ion toxicity. This review examines the complex orchestration of Na⁺ fluxes across various cellular membranes and tissues. While high levels of cytoplasmic Na⁺ can severely impair metabolic processes, salt-tolerant species maintain homeostasis not necessarily through novel transport proteins, but through the sophisticated regulation of existing flux pathways. We discuss the key mechanisms involved in Na⁺ entry via non-selective cation channels and HKT-type transporters, as well as its active extrusion and vacuolar sequestration mediated by Na⁺/H⁺ antiporters such as SOS1 and NHX1. Additionally, the paper addresses long-distance transport, including xylem loading, retrieval by HKT1;1-type proteins, and the physiological significance of phloem recirculation and specialized salt-secreting organs. We synthesis current knowledge on the signaling cascades—including calcium transients, cyclic nucleotides (cGMP), reactive oxygen species, and hormonal cues—that translate environmental salt sensing into transcriptional and post-translational adjustments. By summarizing these early-stage regulatory strategies, this work highlights the potential for engineering salt resilience in crops through the targeted manipulation of regulatory networks rather than single-gene traits.
